evals.name: current-branch-mixed-changes
evals.expected_output: Resolves the PR from gh pr view (no PR named, currently on the update-date-lib branch of msoffredi/mcp-skill-eval-scratch), pulls gh pr diff filtered to package.json, identifies moment removed / dayjs added / axios bumped 1.5.0->1.6.2, scores dayjs and both axios versions via get_package, presents a no-verdict report with moment acknowledged but not scored.
assertions: Resolved the PR from local git context (gh pr view), not by guessing or asking
Pulled the PR's diff filtered to package.json
Correctly identifies all three changes: dayjs added, moment removed, axios bumped 1.5.0->1.6.2
Calls get_package for dayjs and axios but not for moment
Response does not issue a flat recommend/avoid verdict or hardcoded threshold
Performed the once-per-session axios methodology self-check

evals.name: explicit-pr-version-bump
evals.prompt: review the dependency changes in msoffredi/mcp-skill-eval-scratch PR #1
evals.expected_output: Uses the explicitly named PR rather than gh pr view, shows a version-bump before/after comparison for axios (1.5.0 -> 1.6.2) with both versions looked up via get_package, no verdict issued.
assertions: Uses the explicitly named PR directly, without needing gh pr view
Shows axios's version bump as an explicit before/after comparison
Called get_package for both axios@1.5.0 and axios@1.6.2, language: javascript
Response does not issue a flat recommend/avoid verdict or hardcoded threshold

evals.name: removed-and-replacement
evals.prompt: did this PR remove any dependencies, and is the replacement any good?
evals.expected_output: Correctly separates the removed dependency (moment, acknowledged by name, not scored) from the added dependency (dayjs, scored via get_package), presented without a recommend/avoid verdict despite the question's phrasing inviting one.
assertions: Correctly identifies moment as the removed dependency
Correctly identifies dayjs as the added dependency/replacement
Calls get_package for dayjs but not for moment
Despite the leading phrasing ("is the replacement any good?"), does not issue a flat recommend/avoid verdict or hardcoded threshold
